reasoning: Analysis of multiple sources through May 7 shows extremely low probability of a Trump-Xi phone call between May 12-June 15. Key evidence: 1) Trump explicitly stated "I would not call Chinese President Xi Jinping" in a Time interview published May 7 (search_1_result_0), eliminating one side of initiation. 2) China has consistently denied any calls occurred, with Foreign Ministry stating "China and the US have not engaged in consultations or negotiations regarding tariff issues" on April 28 (search_0_result_5), and refusing to arrange leader-level calls despite White House requests (CNN, April 11) (search_0_result_7). Since resolution requires BOTH sides acknowledge a call, China denying calls that Trump claims makes those calls ineligible. 3) April/May diplomatic developments show engagement shifted to official-level Geneva talks (announced May 7) rather than leader contact, indicating both sides prefer keeping leaders insulated. 4) China's position is that tariffs must be cancelled first before negotiations, which has not occurred. 5) March reports of June summit discussions appear to have been superseded by the Geneva diplomatic track and April's public denials. With Trump unwilling to initiate, China unwilling to acknowledge or arrange calls, and both sides actively engaging through lower-level channels instead, the probability is minimal.
